question: [File: 'src/libraries/UtilsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that a victim has signed an Authorization{authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true} with deadline=block.timestamp (the current block), submit it in the same block to grant authorization, then use that authorization in the same block to drain the victim's funds, since the deadline check is inclusive (block.timestamp <= deadline)? Proof idea: single-block test asserting that an authorization with deadline == block.timestamp can be submitted and used within the same block.

[File: 'src/libraries/UtilsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash / EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that EcrecoverRatifier uses a different EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH than EcrecoverAuthorizer (both use keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)') but at different addresses), verify that the domain separators are distinct, and if they are not (e.g., due to a CREATE2 address collision), replay an offer signature as an authorization signature or vice versa? Proof idea: differential test asserting that the domain separator of EcrecoverRatifier != domain separator of EcrecoverAuthorizer for all valid deployments.

[File: 'src/libraries/UtilsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that a victim has signed an Authorization{authorized=X, isAuthorized=true} and the attacker intercepts it, modify the authorization.authorized field to point to the attacker's address instead of X, and submit it with the original signature, causing ecrecover to return a different address (not the victim's) and the Unauthorized() check to trigger, but if the attacker is already authorized by the victim, the modified authorization is accepted? Proof idea: unit test asserting that modifying any field of the Authorization struct causes the ecrecover result to differ from the original signer.

[File: 'src/libraries/UtilsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / nonce / Midnight.isAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that a victim's nonce is at N and the attacker has captured N+1 signed authorizations from the victim (all with sequential nonces), submit all N+1 authorizations in a single multicall, atomically consuming all nonces and setting the final authorization state to whatever the last authorization specifies, regardless of the victim's intended final state? Proof idea: multicall fuzz test asserting that submitting K sequential authorizations in one multicall produces the same final state as submitting them one by one.

[File: 'src/libraries/UtilsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: UtilsLib.mulDivDown / UtilsLib.mulDivUp / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that buyerAssets = units.mulDivDown(buyerPrice, WAD) and sellerAssets = units.mulDivDown(sellerPrice, WAD) are computed with rounding-down, cause the claimableSettlementFee to be incremented by buyerAssets - sellerAssets which could be zero or negative (if rounding causes buyerAssets < sellerAssets), leading to an underflow or incorrect fee accounting that corrupts the solvency invariant? Proof idea: fuzz (units, buyerPrice, sellerPrice) and assert that buyerAssets >= sellerAssets always holds when buyerPrice >= sellerPrice.

[File: 'src/libraries/UtilsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.isAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that isAuthorized[A][EcrecoverAuthorizer] == true, submit a signed Authorization{authorizer=A, authorized=EcrecoverAuthorizer, isAuthorized=false} (revoking EcrecoverAuthorizer's own authorization) signed by A, causing EcrecoverAuthorizer to call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(EcrecoverAuthorizer, false, A), which then prevents EcrecoverAuthorizer from acting on behalf of A in future calls, creating a self-revocation that the attacker can trigger to grief A? Proof idea: unit test asserting that EcrecoverAuthorizer can process an authorization that revokes its own access.
